vitals.field_size: 870 registered against a 1,500-rider cap (2026); historically much larger — roughly 5,900 in 2019 and 7,500 advertised in 2018

climate.description: Race-day forecasts point to a sharp thermal contrast: warm Chiang Mai valley conditions give way to a much colder, frequently foggy summit zone above 1,500m. The 8 Feb 2026 Thai Meteorological Department forecast called for roughly 18-34°C in Chiang Mai and 5-14°C on northern mountaintops — forecast ranges, not measured start/summit readings during the ride.
challenges: Forecast valley-to-summit contrast — Chiang Mai 18-34°C vs. 5-14°C on northern mountaintops per race-day forecasts
Morning fog common on the upper mountain
Damp, narrow, quiet road surface near the summit

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 73. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 69.

course_description.character: A one-way, point-to-point summit race up Highway 1009 to the roof of Thailand, not a steady alpine grind. The first 8km from the start at km 0 is essentially flat warm-up. After the national park gate the road kicks with a short, sharp 1.3km at 7% (touching 13%), then settles into 10km of rolling false-flat. Near km 20 the road climbs 6km at 7% (ramping to 14%) through a forested gorge past Wachirathan waterfall, followed by a brief respite, then 3km at 9% to Ban Khun Klang around km 31. The road eases for two easier kilometers before another 3.3km at 10% (including roughly 800m at 16%). From about km 39, the final 9km averages roughly 9%, with a reported 3.5km stretch at 14% and pitches touching 25% before the summit. The route climbs from lowland deciduous forest through terraced paddies into cloud-covered montane evergreen forest above about 1,500m, passing twin royal stupas near the top.
course_description.signature_challenge: The final 9km from roughly km 39 to the Doi Inthanon summit — averaging about 9% with a documented 3.5km stretch at 14% and pitches as steep as 25-23% above 2,000m, arriving as the air turns cold, damp, and frequently fogged in.
suffering_zones: km 20-26 gorge climb: 6km at 7% ramping to 14%, past Wachirathan waterfall
Final 9km (km 39-48): averages ~9%, with 3.5km at 14% and pitches to 23-25%, arriving in cold summit fog

logistics.airport: Chiang Mai International Airport (CNX) — roughly 60-75km / 1.5-2hr drive to the Highway 1009 start near Chom Thong.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Hotels and guesthouses in Chom Thong and Chiang Mai. Book early for event weekend.
logistics.transport: Self-drive or arranged transport from Chiang Mai to the Highway 1009 start; no official standalone event site, registration runs through the ThaiMTB platform.

history.origin_story: Began in 2008 as a mountain-bike race run on paved roads, organized by the Chom Thong Cycling for Health Association (สมาคมจักรยานเพื่อสุขภาพจอมทอง). Road bikes rode informally in early editions before getting an official road-bike category in 2011 (the fourth running). The event was postponed once for COVID (planned for 2022, held in 2023) but has otherwise run annually, reaching edition #17 in February 2026.
history.reputation: One of Thailand's toughest annual hill-climb events — a summit finish at the country's highest point, historically drawing thousands of riders in its peak years before settling into a smaller, more serious field.

biased_opinion.verdict: A Real Summit Climb, Overpromised by Old Stats
biased_opinion.summary: The Inthanon Challenge is a genuine, currently active hill-climb race to the summit of Doi Inthanon, Thailand's highest point, now in its 17th edition. The database previously carried 48.3km/2,565m as course stats, but 2,565m is the mountain's summit elevation above sea level, not the race's climbing gain — the organizer has never published an official total-ascent figure, and the best route-specific estimate is closer to 2,580m of gain over roughly 48km. The 2026 field of 870 registered riders (against a 1,500 cap) is a fraction of the event's 2018-2019 peak, when it drew 6,000-7,500 entrants; the old 'thousands' field-size label describes a bigger, older version of this race. What hasn't diminished is the climb itself: a genuinely brutal final 9km averaging 9% with pitches over 20%, arriving in cold summit fog after riders start the day in Thai valley heat.
strengths: A real summit finish at Thailand's highest point (2,565m) with a documented, brutal final 9km averaging ~9% and touching 23-25% gradient

Dramatic forecast thermal contrast — race-day forecasts put Chiang Mai at 18-34°C against 5-14°C on northern mountaintops, plus frequent summit fog, a genuine physiological test beyond just the climbing
Scenic route through national park forest, past Wachirathan waterfall and terraced paddies into cloud forest
Long-running event (17 editions since 2008) with a stable organizer (Chom Thong Cycling for Health Association)
weaknesses: The database's prior 2,565m elevation figure was the mountain's summit altitude, not its climbing gain — the organizer does not publish an official ascent total, so treat any total-elevation figure (including this profile's 2,580m) as a route estimate, not a certified number
Field size has shrunk sharply from its 2018-2019 peak (6,000-7,500 riders) to 870 registered in 2026 — a much smaller, quieter event than the 'thousands' framing implies

A 2017 race report documented only semi-closed roads with traffic building up behind the front of the field; 2018 and 2019 organizer records describe road closure, but current 2026 closure status wasn't independently confirmed — don't assume either way without checking the current year

A participant died in a 2018 descent collision with an uphill-traveling truck near km 40-41 — a documented, serious safety incident on this descent in that year, not evidence the event is unsafe every year but a real fact worth knowing
No standalone event website; registration and information run through the ThaiMTB platform, largely in Thai
biased_opinion.bottom_line: If you want a genuinely hard, scenic summit hill-climb to the top of Thailand and can handle logistics run mostly in Thai through a regional cycling association, this delivers real difficulty. Go in with accurate expectations on field size (a few hundred to low thousands now, not the historical peak) and confirm current-year road-closure status and descent safety measures before race day.

final_verdict.one_liner: A genuine, brutal summit climb to the roof of Thailand — smaller and quieter than its old 'thousands' framing suggests.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want a hard, scenic hill-climb race with a real summit finish and don't need a huge field. Prepare for the forecast valley-to-summit thermal contrast and confirm current road-closure and descent-safety measures — the descent has seen at least one serious traffic incident in 2018.
final_verdict.alternatives: For a similar high-altitude summit hill-climb in Southeast Asia, look at other ThaiMTB-calendar mountain events. For a bigger-field Thai cycling experience, look toward Thailand's larger regional gran fondos.

eligibility.notes: Mass-participation road cycling climb at Doi Inthanon; edition #17 held 8 Feb 2026 with 870 registered against a 1,500-rider cap, no indication of discontinuation. Organizer is the Chom Thong Cycling for Health Association; founded 2008 (not ~2009-2010 as previously estimated). Distance corrected to 48km (organizer-advertised, not 48.3km); elevation corrected to ~2,580m route-estimate climbing gain — the prior 2,565m figure was the summit's elevation above sea level, not a verified ascent total (organizer does not publish one). Verified 2026-07-24 via codex gpt-5.6-sol foreground research, editorial wave 4.
